planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Hosta rupifraga is unusual among hostas in tolerating more sun and drier conditions than most species, reflecting its origin on rocky, cliff-face habitats in Japan.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Hosta rupifraga is a small-to-medium Japanese species hosta named for its native habitat — growing on rocky cliff faces and rock outcrops (rupifraga = rock-splitting) in central Japan, a distinctly different habitat from the moist, shaded woodland floors where most hostas grow. The small, ovate leaves with characteristic wavy margins and pale midribs are attractive. Because it evolved on exposed, rocky cliff habitats, it is more tolerant of drier conditions, higher light, and poorer soils than typical hostas — making it a more versatile species for gardens where moisture and shade are variable. The lavender flowers are attractive in late summer. One of the less-known Japanese species hostas, it is increasingly of interest to hosta collectors and breeders as a source of drought-tolerance genes for new hybrid varieties. Forms compact, attractive clumps that can be divided every few years.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in well-drained to moist, strongly acidic, cool, humus-rich, rocky or loamy soil in full shade; mountain wood fern grows naturally in cool, moist, shaded mountain forests of northeastern North America, requiring cool temperatures, high humidity, and consistently moist but well-drained, acidic soils.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in well-drained to moist, strongly acidic, cool, humus-rich, rocky or loamy soil in full shade to partial shade; mountain wood fern is a graceful, medium-sized deciduous wood fern of cool, moist, northern and mountain forests from Newfoundland and Quebec south through the Appalachians to Virginia, growing on shaded rocky slopes, cool ravine walls, and moist boreal forest floors. The arching, broadly triangular fronds have a characteristic spreading habit and typical Dryopteris appearance — pinnate-pinnatifid to bipinnate, with the light green fronds turning yellow before dying back to the ground in autumn. Like other wood ferns, it is tolerant of moderate drought when established in shaded positions, but requires consistently cool conditions and good drainage with adequate moisture. Best in cool, moist shade gardens of the northeastern US and similar cool-climate gardens. Combines beautifully with hostas, trilliums, and other woodland wildflowers in shaded border plantings.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in wet to moist, strongly acidic, peaty or humus-rich, poorly to well-drained soil in partial to full shade; crested wood fern is specifically adapted to wet, boggy, acidic woodland habitats and swamp edges, requiring consistently moist to wet, acidic conditions. An excellent fern for rain garden wet zones and shaded bog garden margins.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in wet to moist, strongly acidic, peaty or humus-rich, poorly to well-drained soil in partial to full shade; crested wood fern is a distinctive native fern of wet, acidic habitats — swamp margins, bog edges, wet forest floors, and poorly drained, shaded depressions from Newfoundland to Florida and west to the Great Plains. The characteristic feature is the pinnae (leaflets) that are held nearly perpendicular (at right angles) to the rachis (frond stalk) and parallel to the ground, creating an unusual, ladder-like appearance quite different from the gracefully arching form of most ferns. The fronds are semi-evergreen, remaining partially green through mild winters. An excellent choice for wet, shaded spots in the garden where standing water or consistently boggy conditions prevent most plants from thriving — crested wood fern actually prefers these conditions and will naturalize in appropriate wet, acidic, shaded settings. Combine with tussock sedge, marsh marigold, and cardinal flower in wet shade gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, strongly acidic, cool, humus-rich, rocky or loamy soil in full shade; rosy twisted-stalk grows naturally in cool, moist, boreal and mountain forests of northeastern North America, requiring cool temperatures and consistently moist, acidic, shaded conditions. Not suited to hot, dry climates.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, strongly acidic, cool, humus-rich, rocky or loamy soil in full shade; rosy twisted-stalk is a charming woodland native of cool, moist northeastern forests and mountain woods — a slender, arching, leafy-stemmed perennial with leaves clasping the zigzagging stem and small, rose-pink to rose-purple, nodding, bell-shaped flowers that dangle from distinctively kinked or twisted pedicels (flower stalks) in late spring. The twisting of the flower pedicel gives the entire genus its name (Streptopus = twisted foot). The flowers are followed by bright red, round berries in late summer — attractive to thrushes, waxwings, and other fruit-eating birds. Like many cool-forest species, it requires cool, moist, consistently shaded conditions and is not adaptable to hot-summer gardens. An excellent native woodland species for naturalistic shade gardens in cool, northern or mountain climates, where it combines beautifully with trilliums, wild gingers, and wood ferns.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, strongly acidic, cool, humus-rich, rocky or loamy soil in partial to full shade; clasping twisted-stalk is a plant of cool, moist, boreal and mountain forests from Alaska and Canada south through the Rocky Mountains and northern Appalachians, requiring consistently cool, moist, shaded conditions.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, strongly acidic, cool, humus-rich, rocky or loamy soil in partial to full shade; clasping twisted-stalk is the larger and more widespread of the two North American twisted-stalk species, growing from Alaska and Canada through mountain forests south to California, Arizona, New Mexico, and the Appalachians. The leaves are heart-shaped, with the base clasping the stem (hence 'clasping' and the species name 'amplexifolius'), creating an attractive, layered, overlapping arrangement along the arching, multiple-branched stems. The small, cream-white to greenish-white flowers hang from twisted pedicels in the leaf axils in early summer, followed by oval red berries that smell and taste mildly of cucumber (earning the common name 'cucumber root'). The berries were eaten raw and cooked by Indigenous peoples across the range. Young shoots can be eaten like asparagus. A larger and more imposing woodland plant than rosy twisted-stalk, more tolerant of a wider elevation range and making a bold statement in woodland garden settings.
